summary: Jeff Atwood critiques Eric S. Raymond's famous 'How To Ask Questions The Smart Way' FAQ, highlighting its ironic futility. The 32-page document demands that newcomers read it before asking questions, yet the very people who need it most would never find or read such a lengthy document. Atwood argues this represents a fundamental misunderstanding of user behavior that plagues the open source community. Rather than berating users into compliance, developers should create software that tolerates mistakes and guides users naturally. The post questions the true purpose of such gatekeeping documents when their intended audience will never see them.
key_points: Raymond's 'smart questions' FAQ is over 10,000 words long—an absurd length for something users must supposedly read before asking any technical question
The FAQ's hostile disclaimer calling confused newcomers 'idiots' and 'losers' reveals contempt rather than helpfulness
Users who ask clueless questions are precisely the ones who won't find or read a question FAQ, making the entire document self-defeating
The open source community's struggles with usability stem from trying to fix users through hostility rather than building tolerant, guiding software
The document's true audience is unclear—it certainly isn't the newcomers it claims to address
money_quotes: It's 32 pages, 1,066 paragraphs, 10,289 words, and 51,757 characters. That you must read before you ask a technical question of any kind.
Users who ask clueless questions haven't the foggiest idea where to find a question FAQ – and even if they did, they certainly wouldn't have the patience to read it.
You can't fix users by berating and browbeating them into better modes of behavior.
What you can do, however, is develop software that is tolerant of mistakes, software that gently guides users in the right direction.
This speaks volumes about the difficulty the open source community has had delivering usable software.
If we know the intended audience will probably never see it, who exactly was How To Ask Questions The Right Way written for?
key_insight: Software communities can't improve user behavior through intimidating documentation; they must build systems that gracefully handle and guide confused users.
